title: Restoring Confidence in the World Anti-Doping Agency Act of 2025
short_title: Restoring Confidence in the World Anti-Doping Agency Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
plain_summary: This bill makes two main changes to how the United States handles Olympic sports and drug testing. First, it officially changes the name of the US Olympic Committee to include Paralympic athletes, calling it the US Olympic and Paralympic Committee. Second, it gives a government office new power over the World Anti-Doping Agency, which is the group that makes sure athletes don't cheat by using drugs. The government office must make sure the United States and individual athletes have fair say in how this anti-doping group works. If the anti-doping group doesn't treat the US fairly, America can stop paying its membership fees.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This Act may be called the Restoring Confidence in the World Anti-Doping Agency Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Authority of National Drug Control Policy with Respect to the World Anti-Doping Agency
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Office of National Drug Control Policy Reauthorization Act of two thousand six. The changes update definitions and add new rules about how the United States works with the World Anti-Doping Agency (a global organization that fights the use of performance-enhancing drugs in sports). First, the changes update the name from United States Olympic Committee to United States Olympic and Paralympic Committee throughout the law. The law also adds a new definition for independent athlete, which means an Olympic or Paralympic athlete who does not work for the International Olympic Committee, the International Paralympic Committee, international sports organizations, the United States Olympic and Paralympic Committee, or the World Anti-Doping Agency. The changes add new responsibilities for the Office of National Drug Control Policy. This office must work with the United States Anti-Doping Agency, the United States Olympic and Paralympic Committee, and the Team USA Athletes Commission to ensure several things happen. The World Anti-Doping Agency must have a trustworthy and independent way of making decisions that gives the United States fair representation. The agency must also fully put in place all needed reforms, including proper rules about conflicts of interest for all members of its Executive Committee, Foundation Board, and all important advisory groups and committees. Independent athletes from the United States and other democratic countries, or people who represent these athletes, must have decision-making roles on the Executive Committee, Foundation Board, and all important groups and committees of the World Anti-Doping Agency. The office must show leadership around the world, have strict standards that work to stop doping in every form, including fighting organized fraud through doping that involves government law enforcement, intelligence or anti-doping institutions, sports organizations, or athlete support staff. The office must also work together with democratic countries. Within ninety days after this law takes effect, the Office of National Drug Control Policy must decide whether the World Anti-Doping Agency meets these requirements. If the agency does not meet these requirements, the office must use all available tools to ensure the United States has fair representation in the World Anti-Doping Agency. Within one hundred eighty days after making this decision, the office must create a report that describes the barriers to participation and fair representation of the United States in all parts of the World Anti-Doping Agency. If the World Anti-Doping Agency does not provide fair representation for the United States, has not fully put reforms in place, or has not allowed the required decision-making roles for independent athletes, the Office of National Drug Control Policy may choose to withhold up to the full amount of membership dues paid to the World Anti-Doping Agency. This decision must be made in consultation with specific congressional committees including subcommittees from the Senate and House of Representatives that deal with consumer protection, appropriations, oversight, and investigations. At least thirty days before the Office of National Drug Control Policy sends money to the World Anti-Doping Agency, the office must submit a spending plan and explanation of how the money will be used to the Senate and House Appropriations Committees.
